title: Programmer, Designer, Human
body: Sami Kallinen is a Systems Designer and Data Scientist based in Helsinki, Finland. He is currently working as a consultant and maintains a keen interest in the intersection of technology, art, and human experience. He can be reached at sami@8-bit-sheep.com or +358 400 129 499.

title: Wire's Best of 2019 vs. Spotify Popularity
body: A data visualization exploring the relationship between critical acclaim and mainstream popularity. This plot organizes the artists featured in The Wire magazine's 'Best of 2019' list according to their Spotify popularity score. It reveals the divergence between avant-garde curation and algorithmic mass appeal.

title: Recruitment Campaign for KSF Media

title: A Historical Heist Is Underway
body:
A major robbery involving tens of millions of euros is currently unfolding. Before diving into the details, let's establish a few principles. Most are familiar with Bitcoin and its underlying blockchain technology—essentially a decentralized database. The core concept replaces a central bank with a shared, encrypted ledger, updated by consensus. This technology holds the potential to revolutionize how we organize society, much like the Internet did decades ago.

Enter Ethereum, often dubbed 'Bitcoin 2.0'. Like Bitcoin, it uses a blockchain, but it also stores code: 'Smart Contracts'. It functions as a massively distributed computer. The DAO (Decentralized Autonomous Organization), launched on Ethereum in April, acts as a decentralized investor-directed fund. It raised over  million, with 11,000 investors voting on how to deploy capital.

However, a vulnerability in The DAO is now being exploited. Hundreds of millions are being drained, crashing the value of Ether. The community is scrambling for a solution. While sociologists might find this unregulated chaos fascinating—technically, nothing stops the fund from financing an invasion—the real story is the fragility of this new frontier. A 'soft fork' may save the funds for now, but this won't be the last crisis of its kind.

(Originally published in Swedish in Hufvudstadsbladet, Finland.)

title: It's the Empathy
body:
Although the Agile Manifesto prioritizes 'people over processes,' it is surprising how often teams overlook the human component. We have conducted daily standups across various organizations for years. They usually work, but not for the reasons many assume. While surfacing problems and sharing knowledge is valuable, the true benefit lies elsewhere.

What matters most is facing our coworkers and making eye contact at least once a day. This ritual fuels empathy. Empathy is the secret ingredient that facilitates communication and prevents the misconceptions that plague organizations. So, keep doing your standups—regardless of the topic—as long as they remain engaging and human.

title: Silicon Valley: Keeper of Humanity's Data?
body:
Automation and robotization are no longer futuristic concepts; machine learning is reshaping our world today. This revolution relies on massive volumes of data, most of which is consolidated in the hands of a few Silicon Valley giants like Facebook, Google, and Microsoft. These corporations have absorbed the world's top AI researchers, effectively centralizing the future of R&D.

This concentration of power raises a critical question: can we trust these organizations with humanity's data? Dan Lyons' book 'Disrupted' paints a disturbing picture of Silicon Valley as shallow, cynical, and irresponsible. If accurate, this culture is ill-equipped to manage such sensitive assets.

Refusing to share data isn't a viable solution; we would miss out on breakthroughs in healthcare and justice. We need this intelligence, but we also need guarantees. Emerging technologies like homomorphic encryption—allowing computation on encrypted data without revealing it—offer hope. Until then, we must remain vigilant about the ethics of those we entrust with our digital lives.

(Originally published in Swedish in Hufvudstadsbladet, Finland.)

title: Open APIs at Yle
body: Yle reached two significant milestones last week. First, we launched a modern, browser-based version of Yle Areena. Second, and perhaps more importantly, this release marks the first step towards a personalized service running entirely on our new Open API architecture. This transition is symbolically huge for the organization, enabling third-party innovation and transparency.

title: The Central Node
body: At one point, five generations of our family walked this earth simultaneously. Today, we laid our grandmother to rest. She was the quiet force—the central node—that held our sprawling family network together, running on pure kindness. Now, only the 'monsters' and I remain of my mother's direct line. We miss her deeply. Photo taken May 30, 2009.

title: Review: Facing Grief
body: A review of the video installation 'Facing Grief' in Helsingin Sanomat. 'Autumn trees photographed from a window are combined with pictures of people grieving... The slight unevenness of the window glass softens the image and makes the everyday outside world feel unreal and unattainable. Such is the world in the midst of great sorrow.'

title: Flow
body: 'Flow' is a poetic and disturbing video installation where subjects float through undefined space, experiencing simplified but intense emotional eruptions. It explores the physical manifestation of mental states. 'Like in Kallinen's other works, extreme sentiments are shown in a candid, moving way.'
